$comment: Curated profiles for notable figures. Portraits use Wikipedia's stable Special:FilePath endpoint (redirects to upload.wikimedia.org). Image filenames verified via WebSearch; bios are short editorial summaries. Names must match keyFigures in events.json exactly.

people.name: Albert Einstein
people.bio: Reframed gravity as the geometry of spacetime in his 1915 General Relativity, after the 1905 'miracle year' that established special relativity, mass–energy equivalence, the photoelectric effect, and Brownian motion. His paper on light quanta seeded quantum mechanics — even as he spent his later years opposing its probabilistic interpretation.

people.name: Aristotle
people.tagline: Greek philosopher; founder of formal logic and natural science
people.bio: Student of Plato and tutor of Alexander the Great. His treatises systematized logic, ethics, metaphysics, biology, and political theory into a vocabulary the West would argue with for two millennia. The medieval and Islamic 'master of those who know'.

people.name: Rene Descartes
people.tagline: French philosopher and mathematician; analytic geometry
people.bio: Anchored modern philosophy on the cogito and proposed a mechanical universe knowable through reason. His Géométrie wedded algebra to geometry, giving every curve an equation and every equation a curve — the language Newton and Leibniz would later turn into calculus.

people.name: Galileo Galilei
people.tagline: Italian astronomer and physicist; father of observational science
people.bio: Turned the new telescope on the sky and watched the Aristotelian cosmos break: moons orbited Jupiter, Venus showed phases, the Moon had mountains. His Two New Sciences founded the modern study of motion, and his condemnation by the Inquisition became a defining episode in the war between dogma and evidence.

people.name: Isaac Newton
people.tagline: English mathematician and physicist; classical mechanics & calculus
people.bio: In the Principia (1687) he unified terrestrial and celestial motion under three laws and universal gravitation, mathematizing the cosmos. Independently co-invented the calculus, decomposed white light into a spectrum, and ran the Royal Mint with the same austere precision he brought to physics.

people.name: Paul Dirac
people.tagline: British theoretical physicist; relativistic quantum mechanics
people.bio: His 1928 equation merged quantum mechanics with special relativity and silently predicted antimatter — confirmed when the positron was found four years later. Co-founder of quantum field theory; legendary for saying nothing unless it was exact.

people.name: Alfred Wegener
people.tagline: German polar scientist; proposed continental drift
people.bio: Marshalled fossil, geological, and climatic fits across the Atlantic to argue (1912) that the continents had once been joined. Mainstream geology dismissed him for lack of a mechanism; plate tectonics vindicated him a generation after he died on the Greenland ice.

people.name: Vint Cerf
people.tagline: American computer scientist; co-designer of TCP/IP
people.bio: With Bob Kahn, designed the TCP/IP protocol suite that turned ARPANET into the Internet — a network of networks that could route around damage and tolerate heterogeneous links. Long-time advocate for an open, end-to-end web; Google's 'Chief Internet Evangelist' since 2005.

people.name: Bob Kahn
people.tagline: American electrical engineer; co-designer of TCP/IP
people.bio: Conceived the open-architecture networking principles that became the Internet, then co-authored TCP/IP with Vint Cerf to realize them. At DARPA he steered the early ARPANET and the National Information Infrastructure agenda that shaped the public web.

people.name: John von Neumann
people.tagline: Hungarian-American polymath; computing, game theory, quantum logic
people.bio: Formalized the stored-program computer architecture that still bears his name, co-founded game theory with Morgenstern, axiomatized quantum mechanics, and contributed to the Manhattan Project's implosion design. Said to think faster than anyone he met — and he met everyone.

people.name: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
people.tagline: German polymath; calculus, binary arithmetic, monadology
people.bio: Independently developed the calculus with the dx/dy notation we still use, sketched a binary arithmetic that would underwrite computing, and dreamed of a 'universal characteristic' — a calculus of thought. His priority feud with Newton soured Anglo-Continental mathematics for a century.

people.name: Louis Pasteur
people.tagline: French chemist and microbiologist; germ theory of disease
people.bio: Showed that fermentation and disease were the work of microorganisms, not spontaneous generation — and invented pasteurization, vaccines for rabies and anthrax, and the modern public-health imagination. Founder of the Institut Pasteur, still a global node in pathogen research.

people.name: John Locke
people.tagline: English philosopher; empiricism and liberal political theory
people.bio: Argued the mind begins as a blank slate written on by experience, founding British empiricism. His Two Treatises grounded government in consent, natural rights, and the right of revolution — quoted at length, two generations later, in the American Declaration.

people.name: Carl Friedrich Gauss
people.tagline: German mathematician; number theory, statistics, geodesy
people.bio: Number theory, the normal distribution, non-Euclidean geometry, the method of least squares, electromagnetism, geodesy — the 'prince of mathematicians' touched almost everything quantitative in the 19th century, often refusing to publish until a result was perfect.

people.name: Marie Curie
people.bio: Isolated polonium and radium from tons of pitchblende, coined the term radioactivity, and won Nobel Prizes in two distinct sciences — physics (1903) and chemistry (1911). Died of aplastic anemia from a lifetime of unshielded exposure to the elements she discovered.

people.name: Pierre Curie
people.bio: Discovered piezoelectricity and the Curie point of magnetism before joining Marie Curie on the radioactivity work that would win them the 1903 Nobel. Killed at 46 by a horse-drawn wagon on a Paris street.

people.name: Steve Jobs
people.bio: Co-founded Apple in a Cupertino garage, then — after exile at NeXT and Pixar — returned to push the iPod, iPhone, and iPad into the world. His insistence that hardware, software, and aesthetics be designed as a single object reset what consumer technology was supposed to feel like.

people.name: Demis Hassabis
people.tagline: British AI researcher; co-founder of DeepMind
people.bio: Chess prodigy turned neuroscientist turned AI founder. DeepMind's AlphaGo, AlphaZero, and AlphaFold redrew the limits of reinforcement learning and protein-structure prediction — earning him a share of the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.

people.name: Archimedes
people.tagline: Greek mathematician and engineer; mechanics & integration
people.bio: Computed areas and volumes by an early form of integration, formulated the laws of the lever and of buoyancy, and engineered war machines that held Roman fleets off Syracuse for years. A Roman soldier killed him while he was tracing figures in the sand.

people.name: Plato
people.tagline: Greek philosopher; founder of the Academy
people.bio: Recorded — and reshaped — Socrates' dialogues, then built a metaphysics in which the visible world is a shadow of eternal Forms. His Academy in Athens trained Aristotle and seeded a thousand years of philosophical schools.

people.name: Ibn Sina (Avicenna)
people.bio: His Canon of Medicine synthesized Greek and Islamic medical knowledge into a single curriculum that Europe's universities still taught in the 17th century. Equally consequential in metaphysics, where his proof of the Necessary Existent shaped scholastic theology East and West.

people.name: Thomas Edison
people.tagline: American inventor; electric light, recorded sound, industrial R&D
people.bio: Held 1,093 US patents and built Menlo Park, the first industrial research laboratory — a factory for invention itself. His electric lighting system, phonograph, and motion-picture camera each spawned an industry; his style of relentless trial-and-error shaped what 'R&D' would mean.

people.name: Blaise Pascal
people.bio: Built a mechanical calculator as a teenager, co-founded probability theory with Fermat, and demonstrated that air pressure decreases with altitude. After a mystical experience in 1654 turned to theology and wrote the Pensées, dead at 39.

people.name: Christiaan Huygens
people.bio: Discovered Titan and the rings of Saturn, invented the pendulum clock, and proposed a wave theory of light that anticipated Fresnel by a century. The bridge between Galileo and Newton.

people.name: Robert Boyle
people.tagline: Anglo-Irish natural philosopher; founder of modern chemistry
people.bio: His Sceptical Chymist (1661) broke chemistry off from alchemy by insisting on experiment and corpuscular theory. Boyle's Law — pressure inversely proportional to volume — is the schoolchild's first encounter with the empirical method he championed.

people.name: John Maynard Keynes
people.tagline: British economist; macroeconomics and demand management
people.bio: His General Theory (1936) argued that aggregate demand drives output and employment, and that governments could — and should — counter slumps with fiscal policy. Architect of the Bretton Woods system that organized postwar global finance.

people.name: Euclid
people.bio: His Elements distilled all of Greek geometry into a deductive chain from five postulates — the model of mathematical proof for the next two thousand years. Only the Bible has been printed more often.

people.name: Cai Lun
people.bio: Court eunuch of the Han Emperor He, traditionally credited with standardizing the use of bark, hemp, and rags to make affordable paper around 105 CE. A technology that — eventually — outran writing on silk, bamboo, and parchment across the world.

people.name: Brahmagupta
people.tagline: Indian mathematician and astronomer; rules for zero
people.bio: First to treat zero as a number in its own right, with arithmetic rules for negative quantities, in the Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta (628). His work reached Baghdad and from there transmitted the Hindu-Arabic numeral system to the Latin West.

people.name: al-Khwarizmi
people.bio: His Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing (c. 820) gave the world the word al-jabr and a systematic method for solving linear and quadratic equations. The Latinization of his name became 'algorithm'.

people.name: Johannes Gutenberg
people.tagline: German goldsmith; movable-type printing in Europe
people.bio: Combined movable metal type, oil-based ink, and the screw press into a system that could produce identical books at industrial speed. His 42-line Bible (c. 1455) opened the era in which ideas could outrun their authors.

people.name: Nicolaus Copernicus
people.bio: His De revolutionibus (1543) put the Sun at the center of the planetary system and demoted the Earth to a planet — a thesis published the year he died, dedicated to a wary Pope. The opening move of the Scientific Revolution.

people.name: Francis Bacon
people.tagline: English philosopher and statesman; the inductive method
people.bio: Argued in the Novum Organum (1620) that knowledge should be wrung from nature by systematic, repeated experiment — and that science was a collective project. His New Atlantis sketched the research institution that the Royal Society would soon embody.

people.name: Adam Smith
people.tagline: Scottish moral philosopher; founder of political economy
people.bio: The Wealth of Nations (1776) argued that division of labor, market exchange, and self-interest — disciplined by competition and law — could produce broad prosperity. His earlier Theory of Moral Sentiments reminds readers that the same Smith took empathy as the foundation of ethics.

people.name: Antoine Lavoisier
people.tagline: French chemist; modern chemical nomenclature & combustion
people.bio: Demolished phlogiston, established that combustion is reaction with oxygen, formulated the law of conservation of mass, and gave chemistry its modern names. Guillotined during the Terror — 'it took them only an instant to cut off his head, but France may not produce another like it in a century'.

people.name: Charles Darwin
people.tagline: English naturalist; theory of evolution by natural selection
people.bio: On the Origin of Species (1859) showed how heritable variation and differential survival could produce the apparent design of living things. Two decades of cautious work on barnacles, pigeons, and earthworms gave the idea its empirical weight.

people.name: Alfred Russel Wallace
people.bio: Independently arrived at natural selection from his fieldwork in the Malay Archipelago, prompting Darwin to publish. Also founded biogeography — the Wallace Line that splits Asian and Australasian fauna still bears his name.

people.name: James Clerk Maxwell
people.bio: Unified electricity, magnetism, and light into four equations and predicted that the EM field would propagate at the speed of light. The 19th century's most consequential physics, and the launchpad for both relativity and the wireless world.

people.name: Dmitri Mendeleev
people.tagline: Russian chemist; periodic table of the elements
people.bio: Arranged the known elements by atomic weight and chemical behavior into a table whose gaps predicted unknown elements — and whose discoveries (gallium, scandium, germanium) confirmed the prediction. Periodicity remains the single most useful idea in chemistry.

people.name: Robert Koch
people.tagline: German physician; founder of medical bacteriology
people.bio: Identified the specific bacteria that cause anthrax, tuberculosis, and cholera, and formulated the postulates that link a microbe to a disease. Won the 1905 Nobel for the tuberculosis work that anchors modern infection control.

people.name: Joseph Lister
people.bio: Persuaded by Pasteur's germ theory, introduced carbolic acid to sterilize wounds and instruments — and watched surgical mortality drop sharply. Antisepsis was the change that made modern surgery possible.

people.name: Werner Heisenberg
people.tagline: German physicist; matrix mechanics & uncertainty principle
people.bio: Built the first complete formulation of quantum mechanics (1925) and stated the uncertainty principle that bounded what could be known about position and momentum simultaneously. Led Nazi Germany's atomic-energy program; the historical verdict on his intentions there remains contested.

people.name: Erwin Schrodinger
people.bio: His 1926 wave equation gave quantum mechanics its second, equivalent formulation — and his cat became philosophy's most famous thought experiment about measurement. His later What Is Life? primed a generation of physicists, including Watson and Crick, to turn to biology.

people.name: Max Born
people.tagline: German physicist; probability interpretation of QM
people.bio: Showed that Schrödinger's wave function should be read as a probability amplitude — the move that gave quantum mechanics its statistical character. Mentor at Göttingen to Heisenberg, Pauli, Oppenheimer, and a generation of the discipline.

people.name: Alan Turing
people.bio: Defined computation itself with the Turing machine (1936), broke Enigma at Bletchley Park, and asked whether machines could think. Prosecuted for homosexuality in 1952; dead two years later from cyanide poisoning.

people.name: John Bardeen
people.bio: The only person to win two Nobel Prizes in Physics: for co-inventing the transistor at Bell Labs (1956) and for the BCS theory of superconductivity (1972). The quiet engine of the solid-state century.

people.name: Walter Brattain
people.tagline: American physicist; co-inventor of the transistor
people.bio: Bell Labs experimentalist who, with Bardeen, built the first working point-contact transistor in December 1947. Shared the 1956 Nobel with Bardeen and Shockley.

people.name: William Shockley
people.tagline: American physicist; the junction transistor & Silicon Valley
people.bio: Pushed beyond the point-contact device to invent the junction transistor, then moved to Palo Alto in 1956 — where his abrasive style drove away the 'Traitorous Eight' who founded Fairchild Semiconductor and seeded Silicon Valley. Late-life advocacy of eugenics tarnished a brilliant career.

people.name: Claude Shannon
people.tagline: American mathematician; founder of information theory
people.bio: His 1948 paper 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication' defined the bit, quantified information, and set the channel-capacity limits every digital system still respects. Master's thesis had already shown that Boolean algebra could be implemented in switching circuits — the bridge from logic to hardware.

people.name: James Watson
people.tagline: American molecular biologist; DNA double helix
people.bio: With Francis Crick, deduced the double-helix structure of DNA in 1953 — interpreting Rosalind Franklin's X-ray data — and shared the 1962 Nobel. Later headed the Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory and the early Human Genome Project; controversial statements about race ended his public scientific life.

people.name: Francis Crick
people.tagline: British molecular biologist; DNA structure & the central dogma
people.bio: Co-discoverer of the double helix, and the theorist who formulated the central dogma — DNA → RNA → protein — that organized molecular biology. Later turned to consciousness, hunting the 'neural correlates' of awareness.

people.name: Rosalind Franklin
people.tagline: British chemist; X-ray crystallography of DNA
people.bio: Her Photograph 51 was the X-ray diffraction image that revealed DNA's helical geometry — shown to Watson and Crick without her consent. Dead of ovarian cancer at 37, before the Nobel that her colleagues would share without her.

people.name: Harry Hess
people.bio: Princeton geologist who, in 1962, proposed that new oceanic crust forms at mid-ocean ridges and is consumed at trenches — the conveyor-belt mechanism that vindicated Wegener and gave us plate tectonics.

people.name: J. Tuzo Wilson
people.tagline: Canadian geophysicist; transform faults and the Wilson cycle
people.bio: Introduced transform faults, hotspots, and the eponymous cycle of ocean opening and closing — the kinematic ideas that closed plate tectonics into a coherent global theory.

people.name: Geoffrey Hinton
people.bio: Persisted with neural networks across two AI winters and helped popularize backpropagation, the Boltzmann machine, and dropout. Co-author of the AlexNet result that ignited the deep-learning era; 2024 Nobel Laureate in Physics.

people.name: Alex Krizhevsky
people.bio: Lead author of AlexNet, the 2012 CNN that crushed the ImageNet benchmark on two consumer GPUs and announced that deep learning had arrived. Co-founded DNNresearch with Hinton and Sutskever; sold to Google in 2013.

people.name: Ilya Sutskever
people.tagline: Israeli-Canadian AI researcher; sequence-to-sequence and GPT
people.bio: Co-author of AlexNet and seq2seq learning; co-founded OpenAI in 2015 and led the research that produced the GPT series. Co-founded Safe Superintelligence Inc. in 2024.

people.name: Jennifer Doudna
people.bio: With Emmanuelle Charpentier, reprogrammed the bacterial CRISPR–Cas9 system into a precise tool for cutting any DNA sequence — sharing the 2020 Nobel in Chemistry. The clinical and ethical aftershocks are still unfolding.

people.name: Emmanuelle Charpentier
people.bio: Discovered the role of tracrRNA in the CRISPR immune system and partnered with Doudna to reprogram Cas9 — work the Nobel committee called 'a tool for rewriting the code of life'. Founding director of the Max Planck Unit for the Science of Pathogens.

people.name: J.C.R. Licklider
people.bio: His 1960 essay 'Man-Computer Symbiosis' and a follow-on memo on an 'Intergalactic Computer Network' sketched the time-sharing, interactive web of machines that ARPANET — and the Internet — would become.

people.name: Hippocrates
people.bio: Severed medicine from divine punishment and miracle cure, grounding it instead in observation, prognosis, and a duty of care. The Hippocratic Oath survives — heavily edited — as the moral skeleton of the profession.

people.name: Ibn al-Haytham
people.tagline: Arab polymath; founder of modern optics
people.bio: His Book of Optics overturned ancient extramission theories of vision, showed that light enters the eye, and ran controlled experiments — one of the earliest systematic uses of the experimental method anywhere.

people.name: Johannes Kepler
people.bio: Salvaged the Copernican system by replacing perfect circles with ellipses. His three laws of planetary motion gave Newton the empirical scaffold for universal gravitation.

people.name: William Harvey
people.bio: Showed by measurement and dissection that blood circulates — pumped by the heart, returning through the veins. His De Motu Cordis (1628) broke a thousand-year-old Galenic dogma.

people.name: James Watt
people.tagline: Scottish engineer; the modern steam engine
people.bio: Added the separate condenser to Newcomen's atmospheric engine, doubling its efficiency and detaching the steam engine from the mineshaft. The thermodynamic core of the Industrial Revolution.

people.name: Edward Jenner
people.bio: Observed that milkmaids who had caught cowpox were spared smallpox. His 1796 inoculation of a boy with cowpox launched vaccination — the only human disease ever eradicated by deliberate medical action.

people.name: George Boole
people.bio: His Laws of Thought (1854) recast logic as algebra: AND, OR, NOT as binary operations. A century later, Shannon noticed those operations were exactly what electrical switches did, and the digital age inherited Boole's notation.

people.name: Gregor Mendel
people.bio: Crossed 28,000 pea plants in his monastery garden and discovered the discrete particulate units of heredity — what we now call genes. Ignored for 35 years; rediscovered in 1900 and christened the founder of genetics.

people.name: Max Planck
people.bio: In 1900, to explain the black-body spectrum, he proposed that radiation is emitted in discrete quanta of energy E = hν — an act he called 'a desperate measure'. That measure became quantum mechanics.

people.name: Edwin Hubble
people.bio: Resolved Cepheid variables in Andromeda and proved that 'spiral nebulae' are other galaxies, not gas clouds in ours. His 1929 redshift-distance relation showed the universe is expanding — Lemaître's Big Bang gained an observational anchor.

people.name: Alexander Fleming
people.bio: Returned from holiday in 1928 to find a stray mold clearing bacteria from a forgotten petri dish. Florey and Chain industrialized the discovery during WWII; together they remade the prognosis of every bacterial infection.

people.name: Tim Berners-Lee
people.tagline: British computer scientist; inventor of the World Wide Web
people.bio: At CERN in 1989 he wrote the first web server, browser, and HTTP/HTML specifications, then convinced his employer to give them away royalty-free. The web compounded what the internet's plumbing had quietly built.

people.name: Panini
people.bio: His Ashtadhyayi gave Sanskrit a complete generative grammar in around four thousand sutras — a formal system that 20th-century computer scientists would recognize as something between Backus-Naur form and a finite automaton.

people.name: Ashish Vaswani
people.bio: First author of 'Attention Is All You Need' (2017), the eight-author Google Brain paper that introduced the Transformer architecture. Co-founded Adept AI and then Essential AI.

people.name: Rudolf Virchow
people.bio: Founded cellular pathology with the principle 'omnis cellula e cellula' — every cell from a cell. Connected disease to lesions at the cellular level and shaped modern medical investigation as a microscopic discipline.

people.name: Matthias Schleiden
people.bio: Argued in 1838 that all plants are composed of cells — the plant half of the cell theory that Schwann would complete a year later for animals.

people.name: Theodor Schwann
people.tagline: German physiologist; cell theory in animals
people.bio: Extended Schleiden's cell theory to animal tissues in 1839, framing the cell as the universal structural unit of life. Discovered the myelin-producing cells of the peripheral nervous system that still carry his name.

people.name: Fritz Haber
people.bio: Synthesized ammonia from atmospheric nitrogen — the fertilizer breakthrough that now feeds roughly half of humanity. Also the father of chemical warfare; the contradictions of his life sit on top of half a century of agriculture.

people.name: Carl Bosch
people.bio: Scaled Haber's bench reaction into a continuous high-pressure industrial process. Without the Haber-Bosch process, global food production would not support the current population.

people.name: Howard Florey
people.bio: Led the Oxford team that purified Fleming's mold extract, demonstrated its clinical power, and persuaded American industry to mass-produce it in time to save Allied soldiers. Shared the 1945 Nobel with Fleming and Chain.

people.name: Ernst Chain
people.bio: Worked out penicillin's chemistry and purification protocol alongside Florey. Refugee from Nazi Germany; co-laureate of the 1945 Nobel.

people.name: Kurt Goedel
people.bio: Showed in 1931 that any sufficiently powerful formal system contains true statements it cannot prove — and cannot prove its own consistency. The result ended Hilbert's program and reset the philosophy of mathematics.

people.name: Noam Chomsky
people.bio: His Syntactic Structures (1957) recast language as a system generated by a finite set of rules from a small core of innate principles — toppling behaviorist linguistics and shaping cognitive science.

people.name: Jack Kilby
people.bio: At Texas Instruments in 1958, built the first working integrated circuit — multiple transistors fabricated on a single chip of germanium. Won the 2000 Nobel Prize in Physics for the idea that ate the world.

people.name: Robert Noyce
people.tagline: American physicist; co-inventor of the IC; co-founder of Intel
people.bio: Independently arrived at the integrated circuit using silicon and planar process — the version that scaled. Co-founded Fairchild Semiconductor, then Intel; the 'Mayor of Silicon Valley'.

people.name: Kary Mullis
people.bio: Conceived the polymerase chain reaction on a 1983 drive up Highway 128 — a way to copy a strand of DNA billions of times in a test tube. PCR became the workhorse of every molecular-biology lab on Earth.

people.name: Craig Venter
people.tagline: American geneticist; first human genome draft
people.bio: Led Celera Genomics in a shotgun-sequencing sprint that finished the first draft of the human genome alongside the public project. Later synthesized the first self-replicating cell with a chemically written chromosome.

people.name: Francis Collins
people.tagline: American geneticist; led the Human Genome Project
people.bio: Discovered the genes for cystic fibrosis, Huntington's, and neurofibromatosis, then directed the public Human Genome Project to its 2003 completion. Long-time director of NIH.

people.name: Katalin Kariko
people.bio: Spent decades on the academic fringes refining synthetic mRNA so the immune system would tolerate it. With Weissman, found that pseudouridine substitution did it — the discovery underneath the COVID-19 mRNA vaccines and the 2023 Nobel.

people.name: Drew Weissman
people.bio: Co-discoverer with Karikó of nucleoside-modified mRNA — the breakthrough that let mRNA be used as a therapy. 2023 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.

people.name: John Jumper
people.bio: Led DeepMind's AlphaFold 2 team, the protein-structure prediction system that effectively solved a 50-year-old grand challenge. Shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry with Hassabis and Baker.

people.name: Confucius
people.bio: Teacher and minister whose disciples compiled the Analects, a manual of ethics, ritual, and government that became the moral grammar of East Asia for two millennia.

people.name: Pythagoras
people.bio: Founded a religious-mathematical school on the conviction that 'all is number'. The theorem named after him predates him — but the idea that the universe could be read in ratios was his.

people.name: Siddhartha Gautama
people.bio: Prince turned wandering ascetic who, after enlightenment under the Bodhi tree, taught the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path. The community that formed around him grew into one of the world's great religions.

people.name: Aryabhata
people.bio: His Āryabhaṭīya gave the place-value decimal system, an approximation of π to four places, the modern sine table, and a heliocentric-flavored explanation of why the stars appear to rotate.

people.name: Eratosthenes
people.tagline: Greek polymath; chief librarian at Alexandria
people.bio: Measured the Earth's circumference from the difference in noonday shadows between Aswan and Alexandria — within a few percent of the correct value. Also gave us the sieve for finding primes.

people.name: Claudius Ptolemy
people.bio: His Almagest gave the geocentric system its mathematical machinery of epicycles — the model the Latin West used for fourteen centuries until Copernicus moved the Sun. His Geographia mapped the known world.

people.name: Sushruta
people.bio: The Sushruta Samhita catalogued 1,100 diseases, 700 medicinal plants, and 121 surgical instruments — including techniques for cataract surgery and rhinoplasty that anticipate modern plastic surgery by two thousand years.

people.name: Claudius Galen
people.tagline: Greek physician; the canonical anatomy of antiquity
people.bio: Dissected animals, treated gladiators, and produced a vast medical corpus that — through the Islamic world and into Renaissance Europe — defined Western medicine until Vesalius started correcting his anatomical errors in 1543.

people.name: Andreas Vesalius
people.bio: His De Humani Corporis Fabrica (1543), based on his own dissections rather than Galen's authority, founded modern human anatomy — and announced that observing the body was now allowed to overrule the ancients.

people.name: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek
people.tagline: Dutch draper; the father of microbiology
people.bio: A Delft cloth merchant who ground better lenses than any university and used them to see 'animalcules' — bacteria, protozoa, spermatozoa — for the first time in human history.

people.name: Carl Linnaeus
people.bio: Imposed a two-word genus-species naming system on living things in Systema Naturae (1735) — the taxonomic spine still used by biologists. Also slotted humans, controversially, into the same scheme as the apes.

people.name: Leonhard Euler
people.tagline: Swiss mathematician; the most productive in history
people.bio: Founded analytic number theory and graph theory, named e and i, gave us the identity e^(iπ) + 1 = 0. Continued publishing after losing his sight; his collected works fill 80 quarto volumes.

people.name: Immanuel Kant
people.bio: Argued in the Critique of Pure Reason that experience is shaped by mind-imposed categories like space, time, and causation. His categorical imperative tried to ground ethics on reason alone. The hinge of modern philosophy.

people.name: Charles Babbage
people.bio: Designed the difference engine and the more ambitious analytical engine — a programmable mechanical computer with separate memory and processor a hundred years before electronics caught up. Ada Lovelace wrote its first programs.

people.name: Alessandro Volta
people.bio: Stacked discs of zinc and copper into the first battery (1800), giving science a steady source of electric current and unleashing electrochemistry. The unit of electric potential carries his name.

people.name: John Dalton
people.bio: Revived the ancient atom as a quantitative concept: each element has atoms of a characteristic weight; compounds combine in fixed ratios. Also the first scientist to describe his own color blindness.

people.name: Charles Lyell
people.bio: His Principles of Geology (1830) argued that the same slow processes acting today, given enough time, produced the rocks beneath our feet. The book that gave Darwin the deep time evolution required.

people.name: Pierre de Fermat
people.bio: A lawyer who did mathematics on the side and changed it. Co-founded probability theory with Pascal, anticipated the calculus, and left the famous marginal note that became Fermat's Last Theorem — unproved for 358 years.

people.name: John Napier
people.bio: Invented logarithms (1614) and 'Napier's bones' to make multiplication into addition. Astronomers and navigators gained the tool that made their tables possible for the next three centuries.

people.name: Joseph-Louis Lagrange
people.bio: His Mécanique Analytique (1788) recast Newton's mechanics in the language of energies and constraints — the Lagrangian formulation that underlies every modern physics curriculum from quantum field theory down.

people.name: Pierre-Simon Laplace
people.tagline: French mathematician; the Newton of France
people.bio: Proved the stability of the solar system over long timescales, founded mathematical probability and Bayesian inference, and proposed that a sufficiently powerful intelligence — Laplace's demon — could predict everything from the present state of the world.

people.name: Joseph Fourier
people.bio: Showed that any periodic function could be decomposed into sines and cosines — the Fourier series that now underpins signal processing, quantum mechanics, and JPEG compression. Also the first to propose the greenhouse effect.

people.name: Sadi Carnot
people.bio: His 1824 Réflexions analyzed the steam engine and arrived at an absolute upper bound on efficiency that depends only on the temperatures between which the engine works — the seed of the second law. Dead of cholera at 36.

people.name: Thomas Young
people.tagline: English polymath; wave theory of light
people.bio: His double-slit experiment (1803) demonstrated that light interferes with itself, vindicating Huygens' wave theory over Newton's corpuscular one. Also helped decipher the Rosetta Stone.

people.name: James Hutton
people.bio: Standing at Siccar Point in 1788, recognized that the rocks beneath his feet recorded cycles of erosion and uplift requiring 'no vestige of a beginning, no prospect of an end'. Gave geology its deep time.

people.name: Michael Faraday
people.bio: Self-taught bookbinder's apprentice who became the 19th century's greatest experimental physicist. Discovered electromagnetic induction, invented the first electric motor and generator, and conceived the field — the language Maxwell would mathematize.

people.name: Ibn Khaldun
people.bio: His Muqaddimah (1377) treated history as a science of social structure and group cohesion — 'asabiyyah' — that anticipates modern sociology by half a millennium.

people.name: Charles David Keeling
people.bio: Began measuring atmospheric CO₂ from Mauna Loa in 1958, producing the unbroken record — the Keeling Curve — that turned climate change from speculation into empirical fact.

people.name: Akira Yoshino
people.bio: Built the first commercially viable lithium-ion battery (1985) by pairing Goodenough's cathode with a carbon anode. Shared the 2019 Nobel in Chemistry.

people.name: Al-Ma'mun
people.tagline: Abbasid caliph; patron of the House of Wisdom
people.bio: Sponsored the translation movement in Baghdad that brought Greek, Persian, and Indian science into Arabic — and from there into medieval Europe. The institutional engine behind the Islamic Golden Age.

people.name: Al-Mansur
people.bio: Built Baghdad as the round capital of the Abbasid caliphate (762) and seeded the libraries and translation workshops that would mature into the House of Wisdom under his successors.

people.name: Albert Michelson
people.tagline: American physicist; null result that launched relativity
people.bio: With Edward Morley, ran the 1887 interferometer experiment that found no luminiferous ether — the empirical wall that Einstein's relativity would later vault. First American to win a science Nobel.

people.name: Albert Sabin
people.bio: Developed the live attenuated oral polio vaccine that displaced Salk's killed-virus shot in mass immunization and brought wild poliovirus to the brink of eradication.

people.name: Alexander Graham Bell
people.bio: Patented the telephone in 1876, then spent the rest of his life inventing — hydrofoils, metal detectors, the audiometer — and teaching the deaf, which had been his original profession.

people.name: Allan Cormack
people.tagline: South African physicist; CT scanning theory
people.bio: Worked out the mathematics for reconstructing internal images from X-ray projections, the theoretical basis of the CT scanner. Shared the 1979 Nobel with Hounsfield.

people.name: Amos Tversky
people.bio: With Daniel Kahneman, mapped the systematic ways human judgment departs from rationality — heuristics and biases that founded behavioral economics. Died before he could share the 2002 Nobel.

people.name: Arno Penzias
people.bio: With Robert Wilson, accidentally detected the 2.7 K relic radiation from the Big Bang while tracking down noise in a Bell Labs antenna (1964). Shared the 1978 Nobel.

people.name: Barry Barish
people.bio: Turned LIGO from a struggling project into the instrument that detected gravitational waves in 2015. Shared the 2017 Nobel with Weiss and Thorne.

people.name: Bernhard Riemann
people.tagline: German mathematician; Riemannian geometry & the zeta function
people.bio: Generalized geometry to curved n-dimensional manifolds — the mathematics Einstein would borrow for general relativity — and posed the Riemann Hypothesis, still the most famous unsolved problem in mathematics.

people.name: Buzz Aldrin
people.tagline: American astronaut; Apollo 11 lunar module pilot
people.bio: Second human on the Moon, 20 July 1969. PhD in astronautics from MIT; later advocate for human Mars exploration.

people.name: Charles Best
people.tagline: Canadian medical scientist; co-discoverer of insulin
people.bio: Banting's medical-student summer assistant who helped isolate insulin from canine pancreases in 1921 — a discovery that turned type 1 diabetes from a death sentence into a manageable condition.

people.name: Charles Kao
people.bio: Showed in 1966 that purified glass fibers could carry light over long distances with low enough loss for telecommunications — the foundation of every transoceanic cable carrying the modern internet. 2009 Nobel.

people.name: Daniel Kahneman
people.bio: With Tversky, founded the program of empirical decision research and gave us the System-1/System-2 framework popularized in Thinking, Fast and Slow. 2002 Nobel in Economics.

people.name: Dennis Ritchie
people.tagline: American computer scientist; C language & Unix
people.bio: Co-created Unix at Bell Labs and designed the C programming language. Almost every operating system, language, and runtime in use today is downstream of his work.

people.name: Edgar Codd
people.tagline: British computer scientist; the relational model
people.bio: His 1970 paper recast databases as sets of relations queried by a closed algebra — the model that SQL implements and that almost every business application still depends on.

people.name: Edward Morley
people.bio: Cleveland chemist whose precision measurements with Michelson failed to find any motion of the Earth through the ether — the negative result that helped launch special relativity.

people.name: Emile Durkheim
people.tagline: French sociologist; founder of sociology as a discipline
people.bio: His Rules of Sociological Method and Suicide (1897) treated social facts as causes in their own right — and used statistics to prove it. The first holder of a chair in sociology in France.

people.name: Enrico Fermi
people.tagline: Italian-American physicist; the first nuclear reactor
people.bio: Built Chicago Pile-1 (1942), the first sustained nuclear chain reaction, then went on to the Manhattan Project. Equally legendary as a back-of-envelope estimator and as the architect of Fermi statistics for half-integer-spin particles.

people.name: Ernest Rutherford
people.tagline: New Zealand-British physicist; the atomic nucleus
people.bio: His 1911 gold-foil experiment scattered alpha particles off something hard and small in the center of the atom — the nucleus. Coined 'alpha', 'beta', and 'gamma' radiation; turned Manchester and then Cambridge into the world's leading nuclear labs.

people.name: Ernst Mayr
people.bio: Architect of the Modern Synthesis that fused Darwinian selection with Mendelian genetics, and the standard-bearer for the biological species concept.

people.name: Evariste Galois
people.bio: Founded group theory and showed why polynomial equations of degree five and above are not solvable by radicals — work he scribbled the night before he died in a duel at twenty.

people.name: Federico Faggin
people.bio: Led the design of the Intel 4004 (1971), the first commercial single-chip microprocessor, and the 8080 that defined the era of personal computing.

people.name: Fei-Fei Li
people.bio: Built ImageNet — millions of labeled images — and ran the annual challenge that, in 2012, vaulted deep learning to the front of computer vision. Co-director of Stanford HAI.

people.name: Ferdinand de Saussure
people.bio: His posthumous Course in General Linguistics (1916) cast language as a system of arbitrary signs that mean only by their differences from one another — the structuralism that would reshape anthropology and literary theory.

people.name: Francois Englert
people.tagline: Belgian theoretical physicist; the Higgs mechanism
people.bio: Co-proposed in 1964 the spontaneous symmetry-breaking mechanism that gives gauge bosons mass. Shared the 2013 Nobel with Higgs after the LHC found the boson.

people.name: Francois Jacob
people.bio: With Monod, deciphered the lac operon — bacteria's switch for turning genes on and off — and gave biology the concept of the regulatory circuit. 1965 Nobel.

people.name: Frederick Banting
people.bio: Led the Toronto team that isolated insulin in 1921. Shared the 1923 Nobel with Macleod, and then half his prize money with Best.

people.name: Georg Cantor
people.bio: Proved that infinities come in different sizes — the diagonal argument that bothered his contemporaries and founded set theory, the foundation that all of modern mathematics now rests on.

people.name: George Gaylord Simpson
people.tagline: American paleontologist; tempo and mode in evolution
people.bio: His Tempo and Mode in Evolution (1944) integrated paleontology into the Modern Synthesis and made evolutionary rates a quantitative science.

people.name: Georges Lemaitre
people.tagline: Belgian priest and physicist; the Big Bang
people.bio: Catholic priest who in 1927 derived an expanding-universe solution to Einstein's equations and proposed the 'primeval atom' from which everything emerged. The Big Bang's intellectual father.

people.name: Godfrey Hounsfield
people.bio: Working at EMI, built the first commercial computed tomography scanner (1971). Shared the 1979 Nobel with Cormack. The Hounsfield unit still labels CT densities.

people.name: Gottlob Frege
people.bio: Invented modern predicate logic in his Begriffsschrift (1879) and tried to derive arithmetic from logic alone — until Bertrand Russell pointed out a paradox in his foundations.

people.name: Henri Becquerel
people.bio: Discovered in 1896 that uranium salts spontaneously emit penetrating rays. Shared the 1903 Nobel with the Curies.

people.name: Henry Briggs
people.bio: Took Napier's natural logarithms and reworked them to base 10 — the form astronomers, navigators, and schoolchildren would use until the slide rule retired.

people.name: Hermann von Helmholtz
people.bio: Formulated the first general statement of conservation of energy (1847), invented the ophthalmoscope, and made fundamental contributions to thermodynamics, acoustics, vision, and electromagnetism.

people.name: J. Presper Eckert
people.bio: Co-designer of ENIAC (1946), the first general-purpose electronic computer, and of UNIVAC I, the first commercial American computer.

people.name: Jacques Monod
people.bio: With Jacob, worked out the lac operon and the broader logic of enzyme induction. His Chance and Necessity (1970) argued that life is contingent and not metaphysically privileged.

people.name: James Allison
people.tagline: American immunologist; checkpoint inhibitor cancer therapy
people.bio: Showed that blocking CTLA-4 releases the immune brake on T cells — a strategy that produced durable remissions in melanoma and seeded the modern checkpoint-inhibitor pharmacology. 2018 Nobel.

people.name: Janos Bolyai
people.bio: Independently of Lobachevsky, developed hyperbolic geometry by dropping Euclid's parallel postulate (1832). The discovery that geometry need not match the world Euclid drew.

people.name: John Backus
people.bio: Led IBM's team that built FORTRAN (1957), the first widely used high-level language. Later co-defined Backus-Naur form, the metalanguage for grammar of programming languages.

people.name: John Goodenough
people.bio: At 57, identified the lithium cobalt oxide cathode that made high-energy rechargeable batteries practical. Shared the 2019 Nobel at age 97.

people.name: John Macleod
people.bio: Director of Toronto's physiology laboratory who hosted Banting and Best's summer work and supplied the resources for it. Shared the 1923 Nobel with Banting.

people.name: John Mauchly
people.bio: Conceived the architecture that became ENIAC, the first general-purpose electronic computer. With Eckert, then designed UNIVAC.

people.name: John Snow
people.bio: Mapped the 1854 Broad Street cholera outbreak to a contaminated pump and stopped it by removing the handle. Founded modern epidemiology and the discipline of evidence-based public health.

people.name: Jonas Salk
people.tagline: American virologist; the first polio vaccine
people.bio: Developed the inactivated-virus polio vaccine licensed in 1955. Famously declined to patent it: 'Could you patent the sun?'

people.name: Jonathan Ive
people.tagline: British industrial designer; Apple's hardware era
people.bio: Apple's chief designer through the iMac, iPod, iPhone, iPad, and MacBook — a two-decade run that re-set the aesthetic vocabulary of consumer electronics.

people.name: Joseph Farman
people.tagline: British geophysicist; discovered the ozone hole
people.bio: Working with the British Antarctic Survey, he and colleagues reported the seasonal ozone hole over Antarctica in 1985 — the observation that drove the Montreal Protocol.

people.name: Karl Marx
people.tagline: German philosopher; political economy of capital
people.bio: His Das Kapital reframed history as class struggle and capital as a self-reproducing social relation. The political and academic afterlives are still arguing.

people.name: Ken Thompson
people.bio: Co-created Unix at Bell Labs with Dennis Ritchie, wrote the B language that became C, and decades later co-designed the Go programming language at Google.

people.name: Kip Thorne
people.bio: Co-founded LIGO and developed the theoretical templates that made gravitational-wave detection possible. Shared the 2017 Nobel with Weiss and Barish. Consultant on Interstellar.

people.name: Linus Pauling
people.tagline: American chemist; chemical bond & two Nobels
people.bio: Founded molecular biology with The Nature of the Chemical Bond, discovered the α-helix of proteins, and won the 1962 Nobel Peace Prize for anti-nuclear advocacy after his 1954 chemistry Nobel.

people.name: Linus Torvalds
people.bio: Began Linux as a hobby in 1991 and watched it become the kernel that runs most of the world's servers, supercomputers, and Android phones. Wrote Git in two weeks because he was annoyed with the alternatives.

people.name: Luis Alvarez
people.bio: Won the 1968 Nobel for particle physics; later, with his son Walter, proposed that an asteroid impact killed the dinosaurs — confirmed by the Chicxulub crater. Also chased the JFK assassination film frames.

people.name: M. Stanley Whittingham
people.bio: Built the first rechargeable lithium battery in the early 1970s at Exxon. Shared the 2019 Nobel with Goodenough and Yoshino.

people.name: Martin Hellman
people.bio: With Whitfield Diffie, published the 1976 paper that broke the assumption that secret communication required pre-shared secrets — and made the public internet possible. 2015 Turing Award.

people.name: Michael Collins
people.tagline: American astronaut; Apollo 11 command module pilot
people.bio: Orbited the Moon alone in Columbia while Armstrong and Aldrin walked the surface. Later wrote Carrying the Fire, often called the finest astronaut memoir.

people.name: Neil Armstrong
people.tagline: American astronaut; first human on the Moon
people.bio: Stepped onto Mare Tranquillitatis on 20 July 1969 with the line that closed the Apollo era's defining sentence. Test pilot's discipline; almost monastic privacy afterward.

people.name: Nicolas Steno
people.bio: Formulated the principles of superposition and original horizontality that let geologists read time from rock layers. Later abandoned science for the priesthood and was beatified in 1988.

people.name: Niels Bohr
people.bio: His 1913 atomic model quantized electron orbits and explained spectral lines. His Copenhagen Institute trained a generation of quantum physicists; his 'complementarity' framed how to talk about quantum measurement.

people.name: Nikolai Lobachevsky
people.bio: Independently of Bolyai, constructed hyperbolic geometry by dropping the parallel postulate (1830). Dismissed for years; vindicated when general relativity needed curved space.

people.name: Orville Wright
people.tagline: American aviation pioneer; co-built the first airplane
people.bio: With brother Wilbur, designed and flew the first successful powered, controlled, heavier-than-air aircraft at Kitty Hawk on 17 December 1903.

people.name: Oskar Morgenstern
people.bio: Co-authored Theory of Games and Economic Behavior (1944) with von Neumann — the book that made strategic interaction a mathematical science.

people.name: Peter Higgs
people.tagline: British theoretical physicist; the Higgs mechanism
people.bio: Proposed in 1964 the field whose excitation — the Higgs boson — gives mass to elementary particles. Watched the LHC confirm it 48 years later.

people.name: Rainer Weiss
people.bio: Invented the laser-interferometer scheme that LIGO used to detect gravitational waves in 2015. Shared the 2017 Nobel with Thorne and Barish.

people.name: Richard Stallman
people.bio: Launched GNU in 1983 and wrote the GPL — copyleft software license that made Linux's permissive distribution possible. Author of GCC, GDB, and Emacs.

people.name: Robert Wilson
people.tagline: American astronomer; co-discoverer of the CMB
people.bio: With Penzias, accidentally detected the cosmic microwave background while debugging a Bell Labs horn antenna (1964). Shared the 1978 Nobel.

people.name: Rudolf Clausius
people.tagline: German physicist; entropy & the second law
people.bio: Coined 'entropy', stated the second law of thermodynamics in its modern form, and made heat a quantitative bridge to atomic motion.

people.name: Samuel Morse
people.bio: Built the first practical electric telegraph and the Morse code that gave it a language. The first global-scale electronic communication network.

people.name: Santiago Ramon y Cajal
people.tagline: Spanish neuroanatomist; founder of modern neuroscience
people.bio: Drew the brain neuron by neuron and proved that neurons are discrete cells, not a syncytial mesh — the neuron doctrine. 1906 Nobel.

people.name: Steve Wozniak
people.bio: Single-handedly designed the Apple I and Apple II — the machines that turned the personal computer from a hobbyist kit into a consumer product.

people.name: Susan Solomon
people.bio: Led the Antarctic expeditions that identified chlorofluorocarbons as the culprit in stratospheric ozone destruction — the science behind the Montreal Protocol.

people.name: Svante Arrhenius
people.tagline: Swedish chemist; first quantitative greenhouse model
people.bio: 1903 Nobel for the ionic theory of dissociation; in 1896 also computed that doubling atmospheric CO₂ would warm the planet by several degrees — the first quantitative climate-sensitivity estimate.

people.name: Tasuku Honjo
people.bio: Discovered PD-1 (1992), whose blockade became the second pillar of cancer immunotherapy. Shared the 2018 Nobel with James Allison.

people.name: Ted Hoff
people.bio: At Intel, proposed that a single general-purpose chip could replace the dozen custom logic chips a Japanese calculator company wanted. The idea became the Intel 4004.

people.name: Theodosius Dobzhansky
people.bio: Founding architect of the Modern Synthesis; wrote 'Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution'. Showed via Drosophila genetics how natural selection acts on real populations.

people.name: Thomas Hunt Morgan
people.tagline: American geneticist; chromosomal theory of heredity
people.bio: His Drosophila 'fly room' at Columbia demonstrated that genes sit on chromosomes — earning the 1933 Nobel and laying down genetics' physical foundations.

people.name: Thomas Kuhn
people.bio: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions (1962) introduced 'paradigm shift' and argued that science changes by discrete revolutions, not steady accumulation. The most-cited academic book of the 20th century.

people.name: Walter Alvarez
people.tagline: American geologist; impact theory of mass extinction
people.bio: Found anomalous iridium in the K-Pg boundary clay; with father Luis, proposed an asteroid impact ended the Cretaceous. Vindicated when the Chicxulub crater was identified.

people.name: Whitfield Diffie
people.bio: Co-author with Hellman of the 1976 'New Directions in Cryptography' paper that founded public-key cryptography. 2015 Turing Award.

people.name: Wilbur Wright
people.tagline: American aviation pioneer; the first airplane
people.bio: Older Wright brother; the systems engineer of the pair. The wind-tunnel data and three-axis-control insight that made powered flight workable were largely his.

people.name: Wilhelm Roentgen
people.bio: Stumbled onto X-rays in 1895 and within weeks photographed his wife's hand bones — the first medical image. First Nobel Prize in Physics.

people.name: Willard Libby
people.bio: Developed radiocarbon dating in 1949, giving archaeologists and paleoclimatologists an absolute clock for the last 50,000 years. 1960 Nobel.

people.name: William T.G. Morton
people.bio: Publicly demonstrated ether anesthesia at Massachusetts General Hospital in October 1846, ending the era when surgery required holding the patient down.

people.name: Ada Lovelace
people.bio: Her 1843 notes on Babbage's analytical engine included what is widely regarded as the first published algorithm intended to be executed by a machine — and a vision of computation as more than arithmetic.

people.name: Aksapada Gautama
people.tagline: Indian philosopher; founder of Nyaya logic
people.bio: Compiled the Nyaya Sutras, the foundational text of the Nyaya school of Indian philosophy — a sophisticated system of epistemology and inferential logic.

people.name: Al-Biruni
people.bio: Wrote an empirical study of India, measured the Earth's radius from a mountaintop in modern Pakistan, and produced more than a hundred works on mathematics, astronomy, and geology.

people.name: Al-Razi
people.bio: Distinguished smallpox from measles for the first time, wrote a 23-volume medical encyclopedia, and ran the Baghdad hospital where he sited the new wards by hanging meat around the city to see where it spoiled last.

people.name: Alan Guth
people.bio: Proposed in 1980 that the early universe underwent an exponential expansion that explains its flatness, smoothness, and structure — the inflationary universe.

people.name: Alec Jeffreys
people.bio: Discovered hypervariable regions of human DNA and used them to identify individuals — a 1984 'eureka moment' that transformed forensic science and immigration law.

people.name: Alexander von Humboldt
people.bio: Spent five years exploring the Americas, then five decades writing it up. His Kosmos tried to unite the sciences into a single view of nature and seeded biogeography, ecology, and climatology.

people.name: Alfred North Whitehead
people.bio: Co-authored Principia Mathematica (1910–13) with Russell, attempting to derive arithmetic from logic. Later founded process philosophy at Harvard.

people.name: Amartya Sen
people.bio: Reframed development as the expansion of human capabilities, and showed that famines reflect entitlement failures, not just food shortages. 1998 Nobel in Economics.

people.name: Amedeo Avogadro
people.tagline: Italian physicist; equal volumes, equal numbers
people.bio: Proposed in 1811 that equal volumes of gases at the same conditions contain equal numbers of molecules. Ignored for half a century; vindicated when the law and the constant named for him became chemistry's spine.

people.name: Andre Geim
people.bio: With Konstantin Novoselov, isolated graphene by lifting it off graphite with Scotch tape (2004). Shared the 2010 Nobel — and an Ig Nobel for magnetically levitating a frog.

people.name: Andrew Wiles
people.tagline: British mathematician; proved Fermat's Last Theorem
people.bio: Worked in secret in his Princeton attic for seven years, then announced a proof of Fermat's Last Theorem in 1993 — patched and finalized in 1995. The theorem had been open for 358 years.

people.name: Apollonius of Perga
people.bio: His Conics gave a systematic theory of ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas — the curves Kepler would later identify with planetary orbits.

people.name: Aristarchus of Samos
people.bio: Proposed eighteen centuries before Copernicus that the Earth orbits the Sun. Estimated the relative sizes of Sun, Moon, and Earth from clever angular measurements.

people.name: August Kekule
people.bio: Proposed that benzene's six carbons form a ring with alternating double bonds — an insight he later said came in a dream of a snake biting its tail. Founded structural chemistry.

people.name: Auguste Comte
people.bio: Coined 'sociology' and argued that human knowledge passes through theological, metaphysical, and finally positive (scientific) stages. The positive stage was his program.

people.name: Augustine of Hippo
people.bio: Bishop of Hippo whose Confessions and City of God set the intellectual frame for Western Christianity for over a thousand years — on time, will, sin, and the state.

people.name: B.F. Skinner
people.bio: Made reinforcement schedules the spine of experimental psychology and the Skinner box a textbook fixture. His program lost ground after Chomsky's 1959 review of Verbal Behavior.

people.name: Barry Marshall
people.tagline: Australian physician; H. pylori as ulcer cause
people.bio: Drank a beaker of H. pylori, gave himself gastritis, and proved peptic ulcers are bacterial — overturning a stress-and-acid dogma. Shared the 2005 Nobel with Robin Warren.

people.name: Baruch Spinoza
people.bio: Ground lenses for a living and worked out, more geometrico, an ethics in which God is identical with Nature — too radical for his Amsterdam community, which excommunicated him at 23.

people.name: Bertrand Russell
people.bio: Co-author of Principia Mathematica, pacifist who went to prison in WWI, public intellectual for half a century, and 1950 Nobel laureate in Literature.

people.name: Bi Sheng
people.bio: Invented movable clay type around 1040, four centuries before Gutenberg. The technology spread eastward through Korea and Japan but did not transform Chinese book production the way Europe's metal type would.

people.name: Bob Metcalfe
people.bio: At Xerox PARC in 1973 he co-invented Ethernet, the local-area networking standard that connected every computer in the office and then every datacenter in the world. 2022 Turing Award.

people.name: Carl Djerassi
people.bio: Synthesized norethisterone in 1951 — the first orally active progestin and the chemistry underneath the contraceptive pill.

people.name: Chanakya
people.bio: Advisor to the first Maurya emperor; the Arthashastra he authored is a sober manual of statecraft, economics, and intelligence — Machiavelli avant la lettre, by two millennia.

people.name: Charaka
people.bio: His Charaka Samhita is the foundational text of Ayurvedic internal medicine — an encyclopedic system of diagnosis, pharmacology, and ethics still studied today.

people.name: Christiaan Barnard
people.tagline: South African surgeon; first heart transplant
people.bio: Performed the first human-to-human heart transplant in Cape Town on 3 December 1967. The patient lived 18 days; the operation became routine within a generation.

people.name: Chrysippus
people.bio: The systematizer of Stoicism; without his contribution, Zeno's school might not have survived. Reportedly wrote 705 books, almost all lost.

people.name: Claude Levi-Strauss
people.bio: Applied structural linguistics to kinship and myth, arguing that culture, like language, is generated by deep oppositions in the human mind.

people.name: David Liu
people.bio: Harvard chemist who invented base editing and prime editing — CRISPR-derived techniques that rewrite single DNA letters without breaking the double helix.

people.name: David Ricardo
people.bio: Wealthy stockbroker turned political economist; his Principles (1817) gave economics the theory of comparative advantage and the labor theory of value Marx would later sharpen.

people.name: David Silver
people.bio: Led DeepMind's reinforcement-learning team behind AlphaGo, AlphaZero, and MuZero — the systems that taught themselves to play board games at superhuman level.

people.name: Diophantus of Alexandria
people.bio: His Arithmetica handled equations with integer solutions — Diophantine equations — and was the book in whose margin Fermat would much later scribble his last theorem.

people.name: Edmund Husserl
people.bio: Argued for a rigorous descriptive science of consciousness, bracketing assumptions about the external world. The thread Heidegger, Sartre, and Merleau-Ponty would pull on.

people.name: Edsger Dijkstra
people.bio: His shortest-path algorithm is taught everywhere; his 'goto considered harmful' letter reset programming style; his unsigned EWD memos shaped the discipline's taste.

people.name: Edward Boyden
people.bio: With Karl Deisseroth, developed optogenetics — the technique of using light-sensitive proteins to switch specific neurons on and off in living brains.

people.name: Elon Musk
people.bio: Founded or led PayPal, SpaceX, Tesla, Neuralink, X, and xAI. SpaceX returned heavy-lift launch to commercial scale; Tesla brought EVs to volume production.

people.name: Epicurus
people.bio: Taught that the highest good is freedom from pain and disturbance, and that the world is made of atoms moving in void. The Garden, his philosophical commune, admitted women and slaves.

people.name: Eugene Stoermer
people.bio: Diatom ecologist who, with Paul Crutzen in 2000, gave the present geological epoch the name Anthropocene to mark humanity's dominant role in shaping Earth.

people.name: Florence Nightingale
people.tagline: English nurse; founder of modern nursing
people.bio: In the Crimean War, halved hospital mortality with sanitation reforms documented in polar-area diagrams of her own design — an early infographic that persuaded Parliament.

people.name: Franz Boas
people.bio: Founded American cultural anthropology and dismantled scientific racism with measurements showing that head shapes change in immigrants' children — culture and environment, not race, do the work.

people.name: Fritz Zwicky
people.bio: From the velocities of galaxies in the Coma Cluster, deduced in 1933 that most of the cluster's mass must be 'dunkle Materie' — dark matter. Coined 'supernova' too.

people.name: G.W.F. Hegel
people.bio: Built a system in which Spirit unfolds through history by dialectical contradiction — an architecture that Marx would later invert and modern continental philosophy is still grappling with.

people.name: Georges Cuvier
people.bio: Established that fossils represent extinct species — overturning the assumption that all created kinds still walked the Earth — and laid down comparative anatomy.

people.name: Gregory Pincus
people.bio: Co-developed the first oral contraceptive (approved 1960), the pharmacology that detached fertility from intercourse and reshaped 20th-century social life.

people.name: Grigori Perelman
people.tagline: Russian mathematician; proved the Poincaré conjecture
people.bio: Posted his proof of the Poincaré conjecture to arXiv in 2002–03 and then declined both the Fields Medal and the million-dollar Clay Prize.

people.name: Hannah Arendt
people.bio: Her Origins of Totalitarianism and Eichmann in Jerusalem — coining 'the banality of evil' — shaped how the postwar world tried to think about modern political violence.

people.name: Henry Bessemer
people.tagline: English engineer; the Bessemer steel process
people.bio: His 1856 process blew air through molten pig iron to burn off carbon, making cheap steel by the ton. The technology that built railroads, ships, and skyscrapers.

people.name: Henry Ford
people.tagline: American industrialist; the moving assembly line
people.bio: Put car manufacturing on a moving assembly line in 1913 and the Model T into the hands of his own workers. The labor regime he built defined 20th-century industrial production.

people.name: Hipparchus
people.tagline: Greek astronomer; precession of the equinoxes
people.bio: Compiled the first comprehensive star catalogue, discovered the precession of Earth's axis, and developed trigonometry for astronomical use.

people.name: Ian Wilmut
people.tagline: British embryologist; cloned Dolly the sheep
people.bio: Led the Roslin Institute team that produced Dolly in 1996 — the first mammal cloned from an adult somatic cell — and opened the modern era of regenerative biology.

people.name: Ibn al-Nafis
people.bio: Described the pulmonary circulation — blood passing from the right ventricle to the lungs and back to the left — three centuries before Harvey rediscovered it in the West.

people.name: Ignaz Semmelweis
people.bio: Showed in 1847 that handwashing in chlorinated lime cut puerperal fever mortality by an order of magnitude. Rejected by his colleagues, died — possibly beaten — in an asylum.

people.name: Ivan Pavlov
people.bio: His dog-and-bell experiments demonstrated that physiological responses could be conditioned by association — the foundation of behaviorist psychology. 1904 Nobel for digestion.

people.name: Jabir ibn Hayyan
people.bio: His vast corpus systematized practical alchemy — distillation, crystallization, sublimation, the alkalis — and entered medieval Europe as 'Geber'.

people.name: Jean Piaget
people.bio: Studied his own children to map four stages through which thought develops — sensorimotor, preoperational, concrete operational, formal operational — and founded constructivist psychology.

people.name: Jimmy Wales
people.bio: Co-founded Wikipedia in 2001 after the failed expert-driven Nupedia; the volunteer encyclopedia became the most-used reference work in human history.

people.name: John McCarthy
people.bio: Organized the 1956 Dartmouth workshop that named the field of artificial intelligence and invented Lisp the following year — still the second-oldest high-level language in use.

people.name: John Nash
people.bio: Generalized game-theory equilibria to non-cooperative games — work done at 22 that won the 1994 Nobel. Schizophrenia interrupted his career; the partial recovery was the subject of A Beautiful Mind.

people.name: John Rawls
people.tagline: American political philosopher; justice as fairness
people.bio: His Theory of Justice (1971) revived liberal political philosophy with the 'veil of ignorance' thought experiment — how to design a society without knowing your place in it.

people.name: John Stuart Mill
people.bio: Refined Bentham's utilitarianism and, in On Liberty (1859), gave the canonical defense of free speech and individual autonomy against the tyranny of the majority.

people.name: Joseph Priestley
people.bio: Isolated oxygen ('dephlogisticated air') in 1774 — the gas that Lavoisier would name and use to bury phlogiston. Also a dissenting minister run out of England for his political views.

people.name: Joseph-Marie Jacquard
people.bio: His 1804 loom used punched cards to control the weave — the first practical use of stored instructions, and the direct ancestor of Babbage's punched-card inputs and Hollerith's tabulators.

people.name: Karl Deisseroth
people.bio: Co-developed optogenetics — using channelrhodopsins to control specific neurons with light — the tool that rebuilt circuit neuroscience.

people.name: Karl Popper
people.bio: Argued that what makes a theory scientific is its falsifiability — a criterion that sharpened how scientists talk about evidence. The Open Society (1945) defended liberal democracy against historicism.

people.name: Kenneth Arrow
people.bio: His 1951 impossibility theorem proved that no rank-order voting system can satisfy a small set of reasonable fairness conditions — social-choice theory's foundational result.

people.name: Konstantin Novoselov
people.bio: Co-discovered, with Andre Geim at Manchester, that a layer of carbon one atom thick could be peeled from graphite and was a remarkable two-dimensional material. 2010 Nobel.

people.name: Laozi
people.bio: Traditionally credited with the Tao Te Ching, an 81-chapter poem on the dao — the unnameable way of things — and the politics of wu wei, governing without forcing.

people.name: Larry Page
people.tagline: American computer scientist; co-founder of Google
people.bio: With Sergey Brin at Stanford, invented PageRank in 1996 and turned the idea into Google a year later. Co-founder of Alphabet.

people.name: Larry Sanger
people.bio: Co-founded Wikipedia in 2001 with Jimmy Wales, served as its first editor-in-chief, and proposed many of the editorial conventions that still shape it.

people.name: Leo Baekeland
people.tagline: Belgian-American chemist; the first synthetic plastic
people.bio: Patented Bakelite in 1907 — phenol-formaldehyde resin, the first fully synthetic plastic. Lighter, cheaper, mass-producible material was now everywhere a possibility.

people.name: Leonardo of Pisa
people.tagline: Italian mathematician; Hindu-Arabic numerals in Europe
people.bio: Better known as Fibonacci, his Liber Abaci (1202) introduced Hindu-Arabic numerals and place-value arithmetic to Latin Europe — and included the rabbit problem that named his sequence.

people.name: Li Si
people.bio: Chancellor of Qin Shi Huang; standardized the Chinese script — the small-seal form that gave the empire a single bureaucratic language.

people.name: Louis Agassiz
people.bio: Proposed in 1840 that great sheets of ice once covered northern Europe and North America — the Pleistocene glaciation that explained moraines, erratic boulders, and U-shaped valleys.

people.name: Louis Daguerre
people.bio: Invented the first commercially practical photographic process (1839), fixing images on silver-plated copper. The age of mechanical reproduction begins here.

people.name: Luc Montagnier
people.bio: His Pasteur Institute team isolated the retrovirus that would be named HIV in 1983 — identifying the cause of AIDS. Shared the 2008 Nobel.

people.name: Lucretius
people.bio: His six-book hexameter poem laid out Epicurean atomism for a Roman audience and survived through a single manuscript rediscovered in 1417 — kicking off the Renaissance recovery of pagan natural philosophy.

people.name: Madhava of Sangamagrama
people.tagline: Indian mathematician; founder of the Kerala school
people.bio: Two centuries before Newton, derived infinite-series expansions for sine, cosine, and arctangent — including the series for π that Western mathematicians would attribute to Leibniz and Gregory.

people.name: Mark Zuckerberg
people.bio: Launched Facebook from his Harvard dorm in 2004; built it into a global social platform serving billions and renamed the parent company Meta to bet on virtual and augmented reality.

people.name: Martin Heidegger
people.bio: Being and Time (1927) reframed metaphysics by asking what it means to be at all, and shaped 20th-century continental thought. His Nazi-era rectorship at Freiburg has never been fully reckoned with.

people.name: Max Weber
people.bio: Argued that the rise of capitalism owed something to a Protestant work ethic, that bureaucratic rationality was modernity's iron cage, and that political legitimacy comes in three flavors — traditional, charismatic, legal.

people.name: Michel Foucault
people.bio: Traced how the modern subject was constituted by discourses around madness, punishment, sexuality, and medicine — and how 'truth' is inseparable from the institutions that produce it.

people.name: Milton Friedman
people.bio: Argued that money supply is what drives inflation and that fiscal activism does more harm than good — ideas that shaped Volcker's Fed, Thatcher and Reagan, and the late-20th-century policy consensus. 1976 Nobel.

people.name: Milutin Milankovitch
people.bio: Computed by hand how variations in Earth's orbit and tilt change insolation across latitudes — the Milankovitch cycles that pace the ice ages.

people.name: Mozi
people.bio: Argued for universal love (jian'ai) and against ritualism and aggressive warfare. The Mohists also did early optics, mechanics, and logic — a rationalist counter to Confucian China.

people.name: Niccolo Machiavelli
people.bio: Florentine secretary turned exile who wrote The Prince (1513) as a job application and accidentally founded modern political science — the analysis of how power actually works, not how it ought to.

people.name: Nikola Tesla
people.bio: Designed the alternating-current induction motor and the polyphase distribution system that — over Edison's resistance — became the global electric grid. Late-life experiments with wireless power preceded modern radio.

people.name: Nikolaus Otto
people.bio: Built the first practical four-stroke internal-combustion engine in 1876 — the cycle that still bears his name and the prime mover of the automobile age.

people.name: Omar Khayyam
people.bio: Solved cubic equations geometrically, designed a calendar more accurate than the Gregorian, and — in the West — became famous as the poet of the Rubaiyat.

people.name: Patrick Steptoe
people.bio: With Robert Edwards, produced the first IVF baby — Louise Brown — in 1978, opening reproductive medicine to a still-expanding repertoire of techniques.

people.name: Paul Crutzen
people.bio: Showed how nitrogen oxides destroy stratospheric ozone — work that led to the 1995 Nobel — and, with Stoermer, popularized the term 'Anthropocene'.

people.name: Pedanius Dioscorides
people.bio: Roman army surgeon who catalogued some 600 medicinal plants in De Materia Medica — the standard pharmacopoeia of the Mediterranean world for 1,500 years.

people.name: Peter Shor
people.bio: Devised in 1994 a quantum algorithm that factors integers in polynomial time — putting the entire public-key cryptography stack on borrowed time, and giving quantum computing its first compelling target.

people.name: Philo Farnsworth
people.bio: Sketched the principles of electronic television as a 14-year-old farm boy and transmitted the first image — a single line — at 21. A patent fight with RCA defined his later career.

people.name: Qin Shi Huang
people.bio: Unified the warring states in 221 BCE, standardized weights, measures, axle widths, and the writing system, and ordered the first version of the Great Wall. Buried with an army of terracotta.

people.name: Rachel Carson
people.bio: Silent Spring (1962) documented the ecological devastation of indiscriminate pesticide use and is credited with launching the modern environmental movement.

people.name: Ray Tomlinson
people.bio: In 1971, sent the first networked email between two computers on ARPANET and chose the @-sign to separate user from host — still the convention every address you've ever used follows.

people.name: Robert Edwards
people.bio: Worked out the conditions for fertilizing human eggs outside the body. With Patrick Steptoe, achieved the first IVF birth in 1978. 2010 Nobel.

people.name: Robert Gallo
people.tagline: American biomedical researcher; co-discovery of HIV
people.bio: His NIH lab characterized HIV in 1984 and developed the first blood test, after a priority dispute with Montagnier's Pasteur Institute team.

people.name: Robert Hooke
people.bio: Curator of experiments for the Royal Society; coined 'cell' from looking at cork; formulated Hooke's law of elasticity; quarrelled famously with Newton.

people.name: Robin Warren
people.bio: Noticed spiral bacteria in nearly half of stomach biopsies and persuaded Barry Marshall to culture them. Shared the 2005 Nobel for proving peptic ulcers are infections.

people.name: Seiji Ogawa
people.bio: At Bell Labs in 1990 he demonstrated that blood oxygenation changes are visible in MRI — the BOLD signal that became fMRI, the workhorse of cognitive neuroscience.

people.name: Sejong the Great
people.bio: Fourth king of the Joseon dynasty. Promulgated Hangul (1446) — a phonetic alphabet designed so 'a wise man can learn it in a morning' — to lift Korean literacy out of Chinese characters.

people.name: Sergei Korolev
people.bio: Survived the Gulag to lead the Soviet space program: Sputnik (1957), Laika, Gagarin's flight. His identity was a state secret until after his death.

people.name: Sergey Brin
people.bio: Co-founded Google with Larry Page after their Stanford graduate research on PageRank. Later led Google X and the Alphabet moonshot factories.

people.name: Shen Kuo
people.tagline: Chinese polymath; the Dream Pool Essays
people.bio: Song-dynasty civil servant whose Dream Pool Essays describe magnetic-needle compasses, the discovery of true north's deviation from magnetic north, fossil interpretation, and movable-type printing.

people.name: Shinya Yamanaka
people.bio: Discovered that four transcription factors can revert ordinary adult cells into pluripotent stem cells — an alternative to embryonic sources that won the 2012 Nobel.

people.name: Sigmund Freud
people.bio: Mapped a theory of the unconscious mind — repression, dreams, transference — that dominated 20th-century thought about the self even as it largely lost its empirical claims to neuroscience.

people.name: Socrates
people.bio: Wrote nothing. Cross-examined the politicians of Athens in the marketplace until they put him to death for it. Through Plato's dialogues, his interrogation became the model of philosophical inquiry.

people.name: Stanley Milgram
people.bio: His Yale experiments on obedience to authority (1961) — administering 'shocks' to a stranger on a psychologist's orders — became one of the most-discussed studies in social science.

people.name: Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar
people.bio: Computed in 1931 the maximum mass above which a white dwarf must collapse — the Chandrasekhar limit, the gateway to neutron stars and black holes. 1983 Nobel.

people.name: Svante Paabo
people.bio: Sequenced the Neanderthal genome and identified the Denisovans from a single finger bone — proving modern humans interbred with both. 2022 Nobel.

people.name: Thales of Miletus
