people.bio: First of the Seven Sages; argued that water is the fundamental substance and explained natural events without invoking the gods. The first attempt at physis.

people.name: Thomas Aquinas
people.bio: His Summa Theologica reconciled Aristotelian philosophy with Catholic theology — the synthesis that became official Catholic doctrine and structured medieval thought.

people.name: Thomas Hobbes
people.bio: Argued in Leviathan (1651) that, absent a sovereign, life is 'solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short' — that we accept absolute authority to escape the state of nature. Modern political theory's starting position.

people.name: Thomas Malthus
people.bio: Argued that population grows geometrically while food grows arithmetically — a trap Darwin would later read across to natural selection.

people.name: Thomas Savery
people.tagline: English engineer; first commercial steam pump
people.bio: Patented in 1698 'The Miner's Friend', an atmospheric steam engine used to pump water from mines — the working ancestor of Newcomen's and Watt's later machines.

people.name: Thucydides
people.tagline: Athenian general; History of the Peloponnesian War
people.bio: Wrote history without gods or prophecies — only causes, speeches reconstructed for what each side would plausibly have said, and a chilling realism about power. The model of secular historiography.

people.name: Tycho Brahe
people.tagline: Danish astronomer; the last great naked-eye observer
people.bio: Pre-telescope, observed planetary positions accurate to minutes of arc from his island observatory Uraniborg — the data on which his assistant Kepler would derive the laws of planetary motion.

people.name: W. French Anderson
people.tagline: American geneticist; first human gene therapy
people.bio: Led the team that performed the first authorized human gene therapy in 1990, treating an immunodeficient four-year-old by infusing ADA-corrected lymphocytes.

people.name: W.V.O. Quine
people.bio: His Two Dogmas of Empiricism dismantled the analytic/synthetic distinction and reduction to immediate experience. The most influential American philosopher of the 20th century.

people.name: William James
people.bio: Founded American psychology with his 1890 Principles, and American pragmatism with his lectures of the same name. Brother of Henry; conversationalist of the same caliber.

people.name: Zeno of Citium
people.bio: Taught from the Stoa Poikile in Athens. His ethics — virtue is the only good, the rest is preferred or dispreferred indifferents — would underwrite Roman Stoicism three centuries later.

people.name: Ahmes
people.tagline: Egyptian scribe; the Rhind Mathematical Papyrus
people.bio: Copied the oldest surviving mathematical textbook — 84 problems in arithmetic, geometry, and proportion that document the state of Egyptian mathematics around 1650 BCE.

people.name: Ambroise Pare
people.bio: Replaced cauterizing oil with ligatures and gentle dressings on gunshot wounds, slashing surgical mortality. Founder of modern surgery and military medicine.

people.name: Chester Carlson
people.bio: Invented dry-plate electrophotography in 1938 — a process IBM and others rejected before Haloid (later Xerox) made it a multibillion-dollar industry.

people.name: Christopher Sholes
people.bio: Patented the first commercially successful typewriter (1868) and the QWERTY layout that arranged frequently used letters apart to stop the mechanism jamming. Both have outlived their causes.

people.name: Chuck Hull
people.bio: Invented stereolithography in 1984 — 3D printing of UV-curable photopolymer layer by layer — and founded 3D Systems. The technology that made additive manufacturing real.

people.name: Comte de Buffon
people.bio: His 36-volume Histoire Naturelle gathered everything known about the natural world. Argued the Earth was many times older than 6,000 years — an empirical estimate from cooling iron spheres.

people.name: E.O. Wilson
people.bio: Pioneer of myrmecology who, in Sociobiology (1975), tried to give animal social behavior — including human behavior — an evolutionary framework. The controversy that followed reshaped social science.

people.name: Felix Hoffmann
people.bio: At Bayer in 1897, synthesized stable acetylsalicylic acid — aspirin — to treat his father's rheumatism. Synthesized heroin a few weeks later, marketed initially as a cough suppressant.

people.name: Frank Whittle
people.bio: Patented the turbojet engine in 1930. The first British jet aircraft flew in 1941 — the technology that made the postwar jet age possible.

people.name: Han Fei
people.bio: Argued that strict laws, harsh punishments, and centralized power — not Confucian virtue — keep states orderly. The intellectual blueprint of the Qin unification.

people.name: Hans von Ohain
people.bio: Designed and ran the first operational turbojet (Heinkel HeS 1, 1937) — independently of Whittle's parallel work in Britain.

people.name: Harold Kroto
people.bio: Co-discovered the soccer-ball-shaped C₆₀ molecule (1985) and the family of fullerenes — opening the door to carbon nanotubes and graphene. Shared the 1996 Nobel.

people.name: Herodotus
people.tagline: Greek writer; the father of history
people.bio: His Histories investigated the causes of the Greco-Persian wars and along the way described Egypt, Scythia, India, and dozens of customs unfamiliar to his Greek audience — inventing ethnography as he went.

people.name: Hugo Grotius
people.tagline: Dutch jurist; founder of international law
people.bio: His De Jure Belli ac Pacis (1625) argued that there is a natural law binding sovereigns even at war — the conceptual seed of the Westphalian system and modern international law.

people.name: Imhotep
people.bio: Vizier to Pharaoh Djoser; designed the Step Pyramid at Saqqara, the first large-scale stone monument. Later deified as a god of medicine.

people.name: Jared Diamond
people.tagline: American geographer; Guns, Germs, and Steel
people.bio: Argued in his 1997 Pulitzer-winning book that environmental factors — geography, available species, disease ecology — explain why some societies came to dominate others.

people.name: Jean Bodin
people.bio: His Six Books of the Commonwealth (1576) defined sovereignty as the absolute, perpetual power of the state — a concept that the Westphalian order would soon institutionalize.

people.name: John Martinis
people.bio: Led Google's quantum-computing group through the 2019 'quantum supremacy' demonstration on the Sycamore processor — the first task believably beyond classical reach.

people.name: Joseph Kirschvink
people.bio: Caltech paleomagnetist who proposed the Snowball Earth hypothesis — that the planet froze over completely in the late Proterozoic — and worked out biogenic magnetite in animal navigation.

people.name: Louis Braille
people.tagline: French educator; the braille writing system
people.bio: Blinded at three; at fifteen, devised the six-dot tactile alphabet now used worldwide by blind readers. Posted at the Panthéon.

people.name: Marcello Malpighi
people.bio: First to see capillaries — and thereby close Harvey's circulatory loop. Pioneer of histology and embryology with a microscope.

people.name: Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
people.bio: Identified 'flow' — the state of complete absorption in a challenging task — and built positive psychology around the conditions that produce it.

people.name: Nehemiah Grew
people.bio: His Anatomy of Plants (1682) gave the first systematic microscopic description of plant tissues — and proposed that flowers are the sexual organs of plants.

people.name: Nicole Oresme
people.bio: Graphed quantities against time, anticipated coordinates, argued for the rotation of the Earth, and translated Aristotle into French. Bishop of Lisieux.

people.name: Paracelsus
people.bio: Burned medical textbooks in public, introduced chemistry to medicine, taught that 'the dose makes the poison'. Founded toxicology and rattled the Galenic establishment.

people.name: Pliny the Elder
people.bio: His 37-volume encyclopedia tried to compile everything the Roman world knew about the natural world. Died investigating the eruption of Vesuvius that destroyed Pompeii.

people.name: Richard Dawkins
people.bio: The Selfish Gene (1976) reframed evolution at the level of replicators rather than organisms and coined 'meme'. Later, public proponent of atheism.

people.name: Richard Smalley
people.bio: Co-discoverer of C₆₀ (buckminsterfullerene) and an advocate for nanotechnology and the energy challenge. 1996 Nobel.

people.name: Robert Curl
people.bio: Rice University chemist who, with Kroto and Smalley, produced and identified C₆₀ — the third allotrope of carbon. Shared the 1996 Nobel.

people.name: Robert Putnam
people.bio: Bowling Alone (2000) documented the postwar decline of American civic association and made 'social capital' a household phrase in social science.

people.name: Satoshi Nakamoto
people.bio: Posted the Bitcoin whitepaper in October 2008 and shipped the first node in January 2009 — solving the double-spend problem without a trusted intermediary. Identity unknown; vanished by 2011.

people.name: Strabo
people.bio: His 17-volume Geographica described the inhabited world as the Romans knew it — physical features, cultures, economies — and survived almost intact.

people.name: Su Song
people.bio: Song-dynasty official who built a 10-meter water-driven astronomical clock tower (1088) — the world's first known escapement mechanism, three centuries before European clocks caught up.

people.name: Sun Tzu
people.tagline: Chinese general; The Art of War
people.bio: The Art of War distilled warfare into thirteen short chapters of doctrine — deception, terrain, timing — that have been read by generals, executives, and football coaches ever since.

people.name: Theophrastus
people.bio: Aristotle's successor at the Lyceum. His Enquiry into Plants and On the Causes of Plants founded botany as a systematic discipline.

people.name: Thomas Sydenham
people.bio: Brought clinical observation back into English medicine, classifying diseases as discrete entities. His preference for plain remedies and bedside attention defined a clinical style still recognizable today.

people.name: Zeno of Elea
people.bio: Devised the paradoxes — Achilles and the tortoise, the arrow, the dichotomy — that tortured every philosopher of motion until the modern theory of limits resolved them.

people.name: Zhang Heng
people.bio: Han astronomer-mathematician who built the first known seismoscope (132 CE) — a bronze urn with dragons that dropped balls in the direction of distant earthquakes.
